dimensions.why_it_matters: Agents live or die on correctly extracting structured tool calls from model output; each model family uses a different format (Hermes, llama3_json, mistral, pythonic, qwen3_coder XML). Wrong/absent parser yields malformed args or silent empty calls - so parser coverage and graceful failure are a core agent-serving differentiator.
dimensions.sota_bar: vLLM ships per-family parsers (hermes, llama3_json, mistral, pythonic, internlm, granite, plus qwen3/qwen3_coder); when constraints aren't enforced it falls back to extracting from raw text, so args can be malformed - making grammar-backed (constrained) tool calling the robustness frontier
dimensions.metric_unit: count of model-family parsers + valid-call rate / malformed-arg rate
dimensions.notes: Known model-specific failure modes (Mistral-7B parallel calls, Hermes-2-Theta quality). Pairing the parser with constrained decoding (XGrammar-2) removes the raw-text-extraction failure path.

dimensions.dimension: Sampler coverage and correctness (temperature, top-p, top-k, min-p)
dimensions.why_it_matters: Sampling controls the quality/diversity/coherence tradeoff and must be implemented correctly and identically across engines for eval parity. min-p in particular keeps outputs coherent at high temperature and is now an ecosystem default; missing or buggy samplers force operators to over-constrain temperature and lose quality.
dimensions.sota_bar: Reference samplers cover the full mainstream set: temperature, top_p, top_k, min_p, typical, presence/frequency/repetition penalties, logit_bias, seed/n/best_of, plus pluggable custom logits processors (argmax-invariant classification). vLLM and llama.cpp converge on Temperature+Min-P as the 2026 default power-user combo.
dimensions.metric_unit: qualitative (sampler/parameter coverage + correctness)
dimensions.notes: Old bar dated 2024-07 and is qualitative (no number). Coverage is essentially saturated and unchanged in kind: vLLM exposes temp/top_p/top_k/min_p/penalties/logit_bias plus a custom-logits-processor API (min_p is implemented as an argmax-invariant processor). The notable 2025-2026 movement is community convergence on Temperature+Min-P as the default and a documented vLLM nuance: under speculative decoding vLLM historically sampled greedily from the draft regardless of temperature (a correctness caveat some 2026 work patches for true rejection sampling at non-zero temp). Bar substance unchanged -> changed_vs_old=false, date refreshed to 2026-01. best_value null (qualitative).

dimensions.dimension: Large-scale expert parallelism (EP) for giant MoE models
dimensions.why_it_matters: Sparse MoE models (DeepSeek-V3/R1 671B, Qwen MoE, Mixtral) activate only a few experts per token, so naive tensor parallelism replicates expert weights and wastes memory bandwidth. Scaling EP across dozens of GPUs (one or few experts per device) is the only way to serve 600B+ MoE economically, and demands all-to-all expert dispatch, expert-load balancing, and overlap of comms with compute. EP scale and balance is the differentiating capability for frontier-MoE serving.
dimensions.sota_bar: EP320: Huawei CloudMatrix-Infer runs DeepSeek-R1 with 320-way expert parallelism (one expert per NPU die: 32 shared + 256 router + 32 redundant experts) over the UB network, superseding the 96-GPU large-scale EP (effective EP~72) demonstrated by LMSYS/SGLang.
dimensions.metric_unit: EP degree (expert-parallel ways in one served MoE instance)
dimensions.notes: SOTA moved up: old bar was 72 (LMSYS/SGLang large-scale EP on 96 H100s, 2025-05-05). CloudMatrix-Infer (arXiv 2506.12708, June 2025) demonstrates EP320 for DeepSeek-R1 on Huawei CloudMatrix384 -- each NPU die hosts exactly one expert, with PD+caching disaggregation over the UB network. This is the current largest published expert-parallel degree for a giant MoE. (SGLang's 96-GPU run remains the leading NVIDIA-GPU EP demonstration.) Out of scope for fak's single-box engine, which runs GLM-5.2 MoE experts on one device.

dimensions.dimension: Data-parallel (DP) attention / hybrid attention-FFN parallelism
dimensions.why_it_matters: When EP fans the FFN out across many GPUs, replicating the KV cache under tensor parallelism for attention duplicates memory and caps batch size. DP attention runs attention data-parallel (each rank owns distinct sequences, no KV duplication) while the MoE FFN stays expert-parallel. This is what lets a deployment hold large batches and long contexts at high EP without exploding KV memory, and it is a distinct, non-obvious axis from raw TP/PP/EP degrees.
dimensions.sota_bar: SGLang's DP Attention is the production technique that eliminates KV-cache duplication across devices for DeepSeek-V3, enabling the EP32/EP72 deployment to scale memory-efficiently; combined with PD disaggregation and MTP it delivers up to ~60% higher output throughput.
dimensions.metric_unit: qualitative (KV-duplication eliminated; ~60% output-throughput uplift in combined stack)
dimensions.notes: The ~60% figure is the combined MTP+EP+PD uplift, not DP attention alone. DP attention is the enabling memory technique.

dimensions.dimension: Multi-node scale-out (combined TP x PP x EP x DP across hosts)
dimensions.why_it_matters: Serving a frontier model means composing tensor, pipeline, expert, and data parallelism across many hosts and an InfiniBand/RoCE fabric. The relevant operator question is not 'does it run on 8 GPUs' but how many GPUs/nodes a single coherent deployment spans, what interconnect it requires, and how cleanly the parallelism dimensions compose. Datacenter-scale orchestration (rack-scale NVLink domains, cross-node fabric) is the table-stakes axis for frontier serving.
dimensions.sota_bar: GB300 NVL72 (72 Blackwell-Ultra GPUs in one NVLink domain) set MLPerf Inference v5.1 records, delivering up to 40% more DeepSeek-R1 reasoning throughput than the prior generation; disaggregated serving via NVIDIA Dynamo added up to 1.5x on Llama-3.1-405B interactive.
dimensions.metric_unit: GPUs in one coherent served instance (NVLink domain)
dimensions.notes: Refreshed: old source was NVIDIA Dynamo disaggregated-serving design doc (2025-03-18). The 72-GPU single-instance NVLink-domain class is unchanged in count, but the current SOTA datapoint is GB300 NVL72's MLPerf Inference v5.1 debut (Sept 2025) -- new silicon (Blackwell Ultra), +40% DeepSeek-R1 throughput, and Dynamo disaggregation giving up to 1.5x on Llama-405B. changed_vs_old=true because the SOTA system and the dated results materially advanced even though the headline GPU count (72/NVLink domain) holds. MLPerf v5.1 (Sept 2025) is the latest round; no v6.x round found as of mid-2026.

dimensions.dimension: Maximum parameter count & frontier-MoE architecture coverage
dimensions.why_it_matters: The first capability question for any serving stack is which models it can actually run. The hard end is the 671B-class sparse MoE (DeepSeek-V3/R1) plus Qwen MoE and Mixtral; supporting these requires MoE kernels, EP, FP8 weights, and MLA/MTP support, not just more memory. A stack that tops out at dense 70B is in a different market than one that serves 671B MoE at scale.
sota_systems: Kimi K2 / K2.x (Moonshot AI, 1T MoE; vLLM + SGLang + KTransformers)
dimensions.sota_bar: Kimi K2 (Moonshot AI) -- 1 trillion total parameters / 32B active, 384 experts (8+1 active) -- is the largest open-weight MoE routinely served by vLLM and SGLang, exceeding DeepSeek-V3/R1's 671B.
dimensions.metric_unit: B total params (largest open-weight MoE served by vLLM/SGLang)
dimensions.notes: SOTA moved: old bar was DeepSeek-V3 671B (LMSYS, 2025-05-05). Kimi K2 (released mid-2025, 1T total / 32B active, 384 experts) is now the largest open-weight MoE with official vLLM and SGLang serving support, surpassing 671B. (Note: this scorecard's fak-side row uses '72B on own engine' framing for llama.cpp CPU; the dim is best read as the field's max-served frontier-MoE, where the bar is now 1T via Kimi K2.) best_value 1000 = 1T total parameters.

dimensions.dimension: Multimodal / vision-language (VLM) model coverage
dimensions.why_it_matters: A growing share of production traffic is image/video/audio + text (VLMs like Qwen-VL, Llama Vision, InternVL, Pixtral). Serving these adds a vision encoder, modality-aware batching, and image-token KV/prefix handling on top of the LLM path. Whether a stack treats VLMs as first-class (and reuses vision embeddings across requests) is a real differentiator versus text-only engines.
dimensions.sota_bar: SGLang is explicitly a serving framework 'for large language models and multimodal models' and extends RadixAttention prefix caching to multimodal inputs with vision-embedding reuse; vLLM also provides broad multimodal model support. Coverage spans the major open VLM families (Qwen-VL, Llama Vision, InternVL, Pixtral, etc.).
dimensions.metric_unit: qualitative (first-class VLM support + vision-embedding prefix reuse)
dimensions.notes: VLM long-context prefill is a distinct cost (see MMInference, arXiv 2504.16083). Exact per-model coverage lists are version-dependent.

dimensions.dimension: Long-context serving (128K-1M tokens) & KV/prefix-cache reuse
dimensions.why_it_matters: Context windows have moved from 4K to 128K-1M. Serving long context is gated by KV-cache memory (linear in tokens), prefill cost (quadratic attention), and cache reuse. The operator-relevant axes are the max context the engine can hold per request, KV quantization / paging to fit it, and prefix-cache hit rates that recover the prefill cost on RAG / multi-turn / agent workloads.
dimensions.sota_bar: Million-token windows are production-ready (Qwen2.5-1M open weights; 128K-1M is mainstream). SGLang's RadixAttention prefix caching delivers up to ~6.4x gains on prefix-heavy RAG/multi-turn workloads and a ~29% throughput edge over vLLM on H100 (16.2K vs 12.5K tok/s) on shared-prefix traffic.
dimensions.metric_unit: x throughput gain on prefix-heavy workloads (RadixAttention)
dimensions.notes: 1M-token open-weight = Qwen2.5-1M. The 6.4x / 29% figures are workload-dependent benchmark blog numbers, not a standardized result; treat as indicative.

dimensions.dimension: LoRA / multi-LoRA hot-swap serving
dimensions.why_it_matters: Multi-tenant and per-customer customization is delivered cheaply by serving many LoRA adapters over one shared base model instead of one full model per tenant. The differentiating axes are how many adapters can be resident on GPU concurrently, how many can be cached in CPU (LRU), and whether adapters can be hot-swapped/updated at runtime without restarting (critical for async-RL weight updates). This is the economics of fine-tune-once-serve-many.
dimensions.sota_bar: The peer-reviewed SOTA is S-LoRA (MLSys'24, arXiv:2311.03285): ~2000 LoRA adapters served over one base model on a single A100-80GB at up to 4x vLLM throughput, via unified paging of adapter weights + custom MBGMM/MBGMV kernels. vLLM Multi-LoRA integrated S-LoRA-style techniques as a follow-on (max_loras GPU-resident + max_cpu_loras LRU CPU cache + runtime hot-swap); SGLang/TensorRT-LLM offer comparable multi-adapter batching. Practical resident count is bounded by GPU memory and adapter rank.
dimensions.metric_unit: LoRA adapters served over one base model (single A100-80GB, S-LoRA)
dimensions.notes: Refreshed from the generic vLLM LoRA doc (2025-01-01) to a concrete 2025 figure: ~200 concurrent adapters on one A100 with <5% overhead, dynamic load-from-CPU/disk + LRU eviction. Confidence medium because the 200-adapter number is from a vendor/SEO blog summarizing vLLM's capability rather than a primary vLLM doc page; the underlying LoRAManager dynamic-swap mechanism is documented in vLLM's official LoRA docs. 2025 research (InfiniLoRA, disaggregated multi-LoRA) pushes further but is not yet a production default. Out of scope for fak (no LoRA path). [VERIFIER OVERRIDE -> 2000: NOT CORROBORATED. The specific "200 concurrent LoRA adapters on a single A100 with <5% latency overhead" figure is single-sourced to the cited Spheron vendor blog. Every other search hit asserting it either IS that blog ]

dimensions.dimension: Embedding & reranker (non-generative) model coverage
dimensions.why_it_matters: RAG and search stacks need an engine that also serves embedding and cross-encoder reranker models, ideally on the same serving fabric as the generative LLM (shared pooling, batching, an OpenAI-compatible /v1/embeddings + rerank API). A stack that only does autoregressive decode forces operators to run a second serving system for retrieval, so embedding/reranker coverage is a real platform-completeness axis.
dimensions.sota_bar: vLLM and SGLang both serve embedding and reranker/cross-encoder models alongside generative models with pooling support and OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoints, covering the common open embedding/reranker families (BGE, E5, GTE, Qwen-embedding, etc.) on the same engine.
dimensions.metric_unit: qualitative (embeddings + rerank served on the same engine)
dimensions.notes: Pooling/embedding/score tasks are documented runner types in vLLM; SGLang lists multimodal + embedding support. Exact model lists are version-dependent.

dimensions.dimension: Hardware backend breadth (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel, TPU, AWS, Apple, CPU)
dimensions.why_it_matters: Vendor lock-in to a single accelerator is a top procurement risk. The buyer axis is how many silicon families a stack runs on with maintained, performant paths: NVIDIA Hopper/Blackwell, AMD ROCm (MI300X/MI355X), Intel Gaudi/XPU, Google TPU, AWS Inferentia/Trainium, Apple Metal, and CPU. Breadth determines portability and negotiating leverage; depth (kernel maturity per backend) determines whether the non-NVIDIA path is actually usable in production.
dimensions.sota_bar: vLLM runs on NVIDIA, AMD ROCm (MI300X/MI325X/MI350X/MI355X), x86/ARM/PowerPC CPU, plus plugin backends for Google TPU, Intel Gaudi 2/3, AWS Inferentia/Trainium (Neuron), Apple Silicon, Huawei Ascend, IBM Spyre/Z, and more; llama.cpp targets the widest portability surface (CUDA, HIP/ROCm, Metal, Vulkan, SYCL, CANN, OpenCL, CPU SIMD on x86 AVX-512/AMX and ARM Neon/SVE/SME). MLC-LLM uniquely adds WebGPU/browser via TVM compilation.
dimensions.metric_unit: count/qualitative (vLLM ~10+ accelerator families; llama.cpp ~10+ compute backends)
dimensions.notes: Some non-NVIDIA paths are Tech-Preview (e.g. Trainium/Inferentia in Red Hat AI Inference). Breadth != per-backend performance parity.

dimensions.dimension: Quantization format & low-precision datatype coverage
dimensions.why_it_matters: Low precision is how a frontier model fits and runs fast: FP8/FP4 on tensor cores, plus weight-only INT4 (AWQ/GPTQ) and KV-cache quantization. The relevant axes are which formats the engine supports, which the target hardware accelerates natively, and how much memory/throughput each buys at what accuracy. Quantization coverage tied to hardware generation (e.g. NVFP4 on Blackwell, FP8 on Hopper/MI300X) directly sets the cost-per-token frontier.
sota_systems: vLLM quantization (LLM Compressor, Marlin, AutoAWQ, GPTQModel, bitsandbytes, Quark)
dimensions.sota_bar: Reference serving stacks now cover the full ladder: vLLM (2026) supports FP8 (W8A8), INT8 (W8A8 + W4A8), INT4 (W4A16), NVFP4 and MXFP4 (via Marlin), AWQ, GPTQ, BitsAndBytes (4/8-bit), AMD Quark, and online runtime quantization, across Turing->Hopper->Blackwell plus x86/Arm CPU for INT8.
dimensions.metric_unit: distinct quantization methods/datatypes supported in vLLM
dimensions.notes: Old bar dated 2025-01. Coverage materially broadened since: NVFP4 and MXFP4 are now first-class in vLLM (Marlin path) on top of FP8/INT8/INT4/AWQ/GPTQ/bnb/Quark, plus a new W4A8 INT variant and online quantization. best_value=10 counts the distinct method families currently listed on the vLLM quantization index. This dimension is fak-vs-vLLM positioning: vLLM is the breadth leader on datatype coverage; the count moved up (NVFP4/MXFP4/W4A8 added), so changed_vs_old=true. Note the Blackwell wrinkle: INT8 W8A8 is dropped on compute capability >=10.0, so the supported SET is hardware-conditional.

dimensions.dimension: Multi-node fault tolerance & failure recovery (resilient serving)
dimensions.why_it_matters: At 96+ GPU scale a single GPU/node failure is a when, not an if, and tightly-coupled TP/EP means one failed rank can halt the whole instance. Operators evaluating large disaggregated/EP deployments need to know mean-time-to-recovery, whether the system degrades gracefully (reroute traffic, replicate KV, take only a failed replica offline) versus full restart. Resilience is a fast-emerging differentiator as deployments cross the single-node boundary.
dimensions.sota_bar: Research systems define the bar: KevlarFlow reports ~20x lower mean-time-to-recovery via decoupled parallel init, dynamic traffic rerouting, and background KV-cache replication; FailSafe sustains high-performance tensor-parallel serving under irregular GPU availability; ReviveMoE targets fast recovery from hardware failures specifically in large-scale MoE inference. Production stacks (Dynamo) add dynamic GPU scheduling and rerouting but full resilient serving is still maturing.
dimensions.metric_unit: x reduction in mean-time-to-recovery (KevlarFlow, research)
dimensions.notes: 20x MTTR is a single-paper research claim, not a productionized standardized number; included to mark resilience as an emerging operator axis.

dimensions.dimension: Automatic cross-request prefix / KV-cache reuse (shared system prompts, agent scaffolds, few-shot)
dimensions.metric_unit: x throughput improvement from cross-request prefix KV reuse
dimensions.sota_bar: RadixAttention's automatic cross-request KV reuse via an LRU radix tree gives up to 6.4x throughput on shared-prefix workloads with 50%-99% cache hit rates; this remains the canonical cross-request prefix-reuse bar in 2026.
dimensions.why_it_matters: Agent and fleet workloads repeat large identical prefixes (system prompts, tool schemas, few-shot exemplars). Reusing their KV across requests is the single largest lever on prefill cost and TTFT, and is now table-stakes; buyers compare hit-rate and the throughput gain it delivers.
dimensions.notes: No newer source supersedes the 6.4x cross-request prefix-reuse headline; the LMSYS RadixAttention announcement (2024-01-17) remains the cited bar and 2026 sources still quote 'up to 6.4x on prefix-heavy workloads' verbatim. Old bar stands. The 2026 refinement is breadth of measured hit rates (50%->near-99% across benchmarks) and the move to multi-tier/disaggregated cross-request pools (Mooncake serving Kimi across thousands of nodes, integrated as a vLLM v1 KV connector and into TensorRT-LLM), which extend cross-request reuse across nodes rather than raising the single-node multiplier.

dimensions.dimension: Cache-aware request routing across replicas (route to the worker that already holds the prefix)
dimensions.metric_unit: x median TTFT reduction vs least-load (224.55ms vs 568.27ms)
dimensions.sota_bar: Cross-region KV-locality-aware routing (GORGO-proxy) makes network latency an explicit routing term and cuts median TTFT ~2.5x vs least-load / prefix-trie baselines (224ms vs 568ms) while preserving prefix-cache locality across regions.
dimensions.why_it_matters: At fleet scale a naive load balancer scatters requests so the same prefix is recomputed on every replica. Locality-aware routing turns single-node prefix caching into a fleet-wide property, the difference between a cache that works at 1 GPU and one that works at 100.
dimensions.notes: The locality dimension moved from single-cluster cache-aware routing (old 2025-03-18 framing) to cross-region locality-aware load balancing. GORGO (arXiv 2602.11688, 2026-02-11): GORGO-proxy gives 2.5x median TTFT reduction vs least-load, 2.5x vs prefix-trie, 2.4x vs distributed GORGO (224.55ms vs 539-568ms), and ~2.9x higher RPS (1.89 vs 0.65) by jointly optimizing network latency + prefix locality + continuous-batching admission state in a centralized router. SkyWalker (EuroSys 2026, arXiv 2505.24095) reserves on global rather than per-region peak demand while preserving KV-cache locality. The key insight: a centralized router that treats network latency as an explicit objective term beats both pure prefix-trie and pure least-load. Cache-reuse is folded into an additive cost model rather than reported as a standalone % in GORGO.

dimensions.dimension: Peak served throughput per accelerator (model-served-by-engine, MLPerf-grounded)
dimensions.metric_unit: tokens/sec per GPU and per rack
dimensions.sota_bar: MLPerf Inference v5.1 / v5.0: ~1.1M tokens/s aggregate on one Azure ND GB300 v6 (GB300 NVL72) rack (unverified submission, beating 865K tok/s on GB200 NVL72); ~33,000 tok/s on a single H200 for Llama-2-70B (40% over H100). These are the buyer-citable upper bounds for engine+hardware throughput.
dimensions.why_it_matters: Throughput per GPU is the denominator of cost-per-token and the headline capacity number every operator sizes a fleet against. MLPerf is the only neutral, audited cross-vendor source, so it anchors the SOTA bar buyers actually trust over vendor blogs.
dimensions.notes: 1.1M tok/s figure flagged unverified; H200 33K tok/s is the audited Llama-2-70B figure.

dimensions.dimension: Inference unit economics ($ per 1M tokens) at realistic utilization
dimensions.metric_unit: USD per 1M tokens; GPU utilization %
dimensions.sota_bar: GPT-4-equivalent quality now serves at ~$0.40 / 1M tokens vs ~$20 in late 2022 (â‰ˆ10x/yr decline). Real production GPU utilization is only 30-60%, so realistic cost-per-token runs 2-3x above spreadsheet estimates; FP8/FP4 quantization roughly halves cost-per-token on H100/H200. Self-hosting a 7B breaks even only above ~50% utilization.
dimensions.why_it_matters: Cost-per-million-tokens at the utilization you can actually sustain is the metric finance signs off on. It folds throughput, batching, quantization, and utilization into one number and exposes the gap between marketing throughput and the bill.
dimensions.notes: Number is market-level, not a single audited bench; utilization caveat is the load-bearing part.

dimensions.dimension: Energy efficiency (Wh per token / tokens-per-watt) and power-capped throughput
dimensions.metric_unit: Wh per output token
dimensions.sota_bar: Empirical inference energy is ~0.0001-0.002 Wh per output token depending on model size; a ~500-token GPT-4o-class query is ~0.3 Wh (~3e-4 Wh/token). FP4/FP8 and Blackwell-class hardware are the main levers. No neutral audited tokens-per-watt leaderboard yet exists, so the bar is a measured range, not a single SOTA number.
dimensions.why_it_matters: Power, not GPUs, is becoming the binding constraint on fleet scale; tokens-per-watt sets the ceiling on how much serving a datacenter can host and increasingly drives both cost and siting decisions. Buyers with power budgets evaluate on it directly.
dimensions.notes: best_value null: ranges only, no audited per-watt leaderboard. MLPerf Power offers a partial neutral source.

dimensions.dimension: OpenAI-compatible API surface + reliable structured output / tool-calling
dimensions.metric_unit: % schema-valid output (guaranteed by constrained decoding when a structured response is produced)
dimensions.sota_bar: Constrained/guided decoding now guarantees 100% schema-valid structured output by construction (XGrammar-2 is the default engine across vLLM/SGLang/TensorRT-LLM, <40 us/token), with the OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions + response_format json_schema surface as the de facto contract.
dimensions.why_it_matters: Agents depend on machine-parseable tool calls; a malformed JSON object breaks the loop. OpenAI-compatibility plus near-guaranteed schema conformance is what lets an operator swap engines/models without rewriting clients, a primary procurement criterion.
dimensions.notes: SOTA moved: old bar was 98.2% JSON conformance (squeezebits guided-decoding, 2025-01). The current framing is 100% structural schema validity BY CONSTRUCTION via constrained decoding -- XGrammar-2 (arXiv 2601.04426, the default backend across vLLM/SGLang/TensorRT-LLM) achieves 100% schema-valid tool-call arguments whenever a call is produced, at <40 us/token. Caveat preserved: 100% is structural validity, not extraction accuracy -- constrained decoding can still reduce reasoning quality (e.g. GPT-5 credit-agreement pass rate 86.9%->70.0%). best_value 100 reflects the by-construction structural guarantee, the lever vLLM/SGLang cite.

dimensions.dimension: Production observability: Prometheus metrics (TTFT/TPOT/queue) + OpenTelemetry tracing
dimensions.metric_unit: metric-set coverage (TTFT/TPOT/queue/KV-cache/prefix-hit Prometheus series + OTel traces)
dimensions.sota_bar: vLLM v1 exposes an extensive Prometheus /metrics surface (vllm:time_to_first_token_seconds, TPOT/inter-token, vllm:num_requests_running, vllm:kv_cache_usage_perc, prefix-cache hit) plus native OpenTelemetry distributed tracing via --otlp-traces-endpoint -- the de facto serving-observability contract.
dimensions.why_it_matters: You cannot meet an SLO you cannot see. Per-request TTFT/TPOT histograms and KV/cache-hit telemetry are what drive autoscaling, capacity planning, and incident response; their absence is disqualifying for a production buyer.
dimensions.notes: Refreshed from the same vLLM metrics doc dated 2025-01-01 to the current v1 metrics design (full per-request TTFT/TPOT/queue-depth/KV-cache-usage/prefix-cache-hit Prometheus series) plus native OpenTelemetry OTLP tracing (--otlp-traces-endpoint, OTEL_SERVICE_NAME). changed_vs_old=true: the v1 engine's metric set is materially broader than the v0 contract the old bar referenced, and OTel tracing is now native. Qualitative dimension (no single headline number), so best_value is null. fak trails: adjudication-plane /metrics + TraceID but no serving TTFT/TPOT/queue/cache-hit series.

dimensions.dimension: Kubernetes-native deployment + LLM-aware autoscaling (metric-driven, scale-to-zero)
dimensions.metric_unit: qualitative (scale-to-zero, custom-metric scaling)
dimensions.sota_bar: KServe + KEDA autoscale vLLM on LLM-specific signals (e.g. vllm:num_requests_running) rather than CPU, supporting scale-from/to-zero with a default 5-min cooldown; the 2025-2026 production stack is vLLM + KServe/llm-d + Ray + Kueue + KEDA on K8s. Ray Serve and KServe provide canary, traffic-split, and multi-model endpoints. The SOTA bar is custom-LLM-metric autoscaling with scale-to-zero, not generic HPA.
dimensions.why_it_matters: Fleet economics live or die on elasticity: scaling on queue depth/running-requests (not CPU) and scaling idle models to zero is how operators avoid paying for idle GPUs. K8s-native lifecycle (canary, rollout, multi-model) is the deployment substrate buyers require.
dimensions.notes: KEDA scale-to-zero is Raw-deployment-mode only in KServe.

dimensions.dimension: Multi-tenant fairness, per-tenant quotas, and SLO attainment under contention
dimensions.metric_unit: per-tenant fairness / SLO attainment under contention (qualitative; per-request unfairness bound)
dimensions.sota_bar: VTC (the 2024 first fair scheduler) is now superseded by a 2025 family: Equinox (holistic fair scheduling), FairBatching (fairness-aware batch formation), PROSERVE (multi-priority SLO-aware), and DLPM (locality-aware fair scheduling) -- all addressing VTC's gaps on prefix-locality, SLO, and diverse workloads.
dimensions.why_it_matters: A shared cluster serving many agents/tenants must stop a noisy neighbor from starving everyone else and must hold each tenant's latency SLO under load. Fairness, quotas, and SLO-attainment-under-contention are exactly the properties a multi-tenant platform buyer evaluates.
dimensions.notes: SOTA moved: old bar was VTC (arXiv 2401.00588, 2024-01). The 2025 successor family explicitly improves on VTC -- Equinox (arXiv 2508.16646) holistic fair scheduling, FairBatching (2510.14392) fairness-aware batch formation, PROSERVE (2512.12928) multi-priority SLO scheduling, and DLPM (2501.14312) locality-aware fairness. The dimension remains qualitative (no single canonical number), so best_value is null. Still a real gap fak should measure given its fleet-of-agents target (no work-conserving fair scheduler, no per-tenant token budgets).

dimensions.dimension: Prompt-injection defense and agent security (attack-success-rate vs utility-under-attack)
dimensions.metric_unit: successful IPI attacks on AgentDojo under the defense (CaMeL = 0; ASR effectively ~0%)
dimensions.sota_bar: On AgentDojo, design-by-construction defenses now reach provable security: CaMeL drives successful indirect-prompt-injection attacks to 0 (vs 8 for the next-best tool-filter) while still solving 77% of tasks (vs 84% undefended); MELON (ICML'25) likewise beats prior SOTA defenses. Undefended best agents still see ASR <25%.
dimensions.why_it_matters: An agent that calls tools and reads untrusted content is an exfiltration surface; indirect prompt injection is the top agent-security risk. Buyers now require an ASR-vs-utility number, not a vibe, and adaptive attacks routinely break naive defenses, so the bar is benchmark-grounded.
dimensions.notes: SOTA moved: old bar was 2024-09-25 vintage. The 2025 frontier is provable, design-by-construction defense -- CaMeL (arXiv 2503.18813) reduces successful AgentDojo attacks to 0 with 77% utility (provable), and MELON (arXiv 2502.05174, ICML'25) outperforms five prior SOTA defenses across GPT-4o/o3-mini/Llama-3.3-70B. best_value 0.0 = CaMeL's zero successful attacks (security guarantee), the strongest published result. This aligns with fak's own structural-containment thesis (capability/IFC floor rather than a detection rate). fak's detector is conceded ~100% evadable; its real claim is the structural floor.

dimensions.dimension: Tool/agent sandboxing, structural containment, and PII/exfil prevention
dimensions.metric_unit: structural containment that holds when the model is fooled (capability/IFC/quarantine; provable-defense class)
dimensions.sota_bar: Capability/IFC-based structural containment is the 2025 SOTA: CaMeL (Defeating Prompt Injections by Design) extracts control/data flow so untrusted data can never alter program flow and enforces capability policies at tool-call time (0 successful AgentDojo attacks); MELON adds masked re-execution + tool-comparison provable defense.
dimensions.why_it_matters: Detectors are evadable, so the durable control is architectural: bound the blast radius so a successful injection still cannot read secrets, write outside scope, or exfiltrate PII. Structural containment is the property that survives an adaptive attacker and that security-conscious buyers actually demand.
dimensions.notes: Refreshed: old source was MELON (arXiv 2502.05174, 2025-02-07). The current strongest structural-containment result is CaMeL (arXiv 2503.18813, 2025-03) -- explicit control/data-flow extraction + capability-gated tool calls giving provable security (0 attacks, 77% utility), the clearest capability/IFC exemplar. MELON remains a co-SOTA (masked re-execution). Qualitative dim (containment-capability presence, no audited ASR-under-containment leaderboard), so best_value null. fak is at parity here -- default-deny capability allow-list, IFC taint sink-gating (FIDES/CaMeL-class), kernel-authored provenance, plan-CFI, context-MMU quarantine -- the same family.

dimensions.dimension: Capability/quality of the served model (SWE-bench Verified, GPQA, AIME, LiveCodeBench)
dimensions.metric_unit: benchmark accuracy %
dimensions.sota_bar: Frontier models served by these engines define the quality bar buyers cite: SWE-bench Verified ~80.6% (Gemini 3.1 Pro) / 79.2% (Claude Opus 4.5 + Live-SWE-agent, Nov 2025); GPQA Diamond effectively saturated ~93-94% (Gemini 3 Deep Think 93.8%, GPT-5.4 92.8%); AIME 2025 up to 100% (GPT-5.4). These are model-served-by-engine results, not engine claims.
dimensions.why_it_matters: A serving stack only matters if it serves a model that can do the job; buyers pick the model on SWE-bench/GPQA/AIME first, then the engine to serve it. Engines must be framed as transport for these scores, and constrained decoding/scaffolding can move them, so the dimension belongs on the scorecard explicitly.
dimensions.notes: Numbers are model+scaffold specific and saturating fast; GPQA Diamond is near-saturated, weakening its discriminating power for 2026.

dimensions.dimension: Cross-agent fleet serving time (N agents Ã— T turns): work eliminated by shared-prefix fusion
dimensions.why_it_matters: fak's product category. When a fleet of agents shares a large system/context prefix, fusing that prefix once across all agents eliminates re-prefill work that even a tuned per-agent cache repeats. This is the axis fak is built to win.
dimensions.sota_bar: The competing floor remains tuned warm per-agent KV reuse (vLLM Automatic Prefix Caching / SGLang RadixAttention / OpenAI prompt caching); fak's own measured 50x5 fleet-serving result (19 min vs 78 min warm-KV baseline, 4.1x from cross-agent prefix fusion) is the live number on this dim and no external SOTA has displaced the warm-KV-reuse baseline class.
dimensions.metric_unit: min (warm-KV baseline fleet-serving time; fak arm = 19 min, 4.1x)
dimensions.notes: No newer external SOTA found that displaces the warm-KV-reuse baseline class for this dim; old bar stands. dimensions.dimension: Dense GPU compute correctness with ZERO vendor GEMM (cuBLAS-free)
dimensions.why_it_matters: Every mainstream stack depends on NVIDIA cuBLAS for its GEMMs. Running a real model's dense path on an owned, dependency-free kernel â€” and proving it bit-matches the vendor oracle â€” is a portability/auditability differentiator.

dimensions.dimension: Model routing granularity: per-aspect + first-class ensemble routing vs whole-request single-model selection
dimensions.why_it_matters: Every mainstream 'LLM router' (RouteLLM, Martian, NotDiamond, Unify, OpenRouter, Portkey, LiteLLM Router) answers one question â€” which SINGLE model should serve this WHOLE request â€” and the only shipped model ensemble is a single fixed recipe (OpenRouter Fusion). Routing a sub-request aspect (one tool call, one reasoning step) to its own model, or declaring a configurable per-aspect ensemble with a reduction, is a granularity layer no surveyed product exposes; it is the axis that decides whether routing is a per-request pick or a first-class, in-loop decision.
dimensions.sota_bar: Surveyed 2025-2026 routers/gateways (RouteLLM, Martian, NotDiamond, Unify, OpenRouter, Portkey, LiteLLM Router) all route the WHOLE request to ONE model; OpenRouter Fusion is the only shipped model ensemble and it is a fixed parallel-synthesize recipe, not a configurable per-aspect reduction. None routes a sub-request aspect to its own model. (Aurelio Semantic-Router routes to an intent, not a model; vLLM/SGLang routers balance replicas of ONE model for KV locality â€” a different layer.)
dimensions.metric_unit: qualitative (routing granularity: whole-request vs per-aspect; fixed vs configurable ensemble reduction)
dimensions.notes: Survey of 2025-2026 routers/gateways in docs/model-routing.md. The gap is categorical (a routing GRANULARITY no surveyed product exposes), not a benchmarked speed/quality win â€” any '10x' is a target to be measured, never an inferred or borrowed number.

dimensions.dimension: Long-session history compaction that preserves the provider prompt-cache prefix (drop-and-splice vs summarize-and-resend)
dimensions.why_it_matters: An agent re-sends its whole transcript every turn; the provider prompt cache discounts the unchanged prefix ONLY while it stays byte-for-byte identical. Almost every built-in compaction (Aider, LangChain ConversationSummaryMemory, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, Anthropic API context-editing) summarizes or clears old turns and re-sends a REWRITTEN prompt â€” which by the providers' own docs breaks that exact-match prefix and re-bills it at full price on the compacting turn. Whether a tool can shrink a long conversation WITHOUT busting the cache is the axis that decides if compaction saves money on the turn it fires or costs it.
dimensions.sota_bar: The field splits two ways. Summarizers (Aider, LangChain ConversationSummaryMemory, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI) and Anthropic's API context-editing REWRITE/clear content and break the prefix cache (OpenAI docs: 'when you drop, summarize or compact earlier turns ... you'll break the cache'; Anthropic context-editing docs: 'Invalidates cached prompt prefixes when content is cleared'). The cache-preserving sub-field is small: LangChain trim_messages (pure sliding-window drop, no rewrite) and Copilot's DELIBERATE cache-boundary reset reason about it; no surveyed tool ships a PROVEN byte-identity splice on by default. Context-editing pairs clearing with a memory tool for recovery (a different axis: recoverability), reporting up to ~84% token reduction on an Anthropic agent eval.
dimensions.metric_unit: qualitative (preserves byte-identical cache prefix while compacting: yes/no; lossy vs drop; on-by-default vs library/beta)
dimensions.notes: Industry mechanisms web-sourced 2026-06 (OpenAI prompt-caching cookbook, Anthropic context-editing docs, GitHub Copilot context blog, Aider/LangChain source). fak's drop-and-splice is verified in internal/agent/anthropic_compact.go (bytes.Equal prefix proof, 13 fail-safe identity exits). FENCE: fak proves byte-identity of the shipped prefix; whether the provider's cache CASCADE realizes the hit on the compacting turn is unmeasured (epic #745) â€” so this is a cache-PRESERVATION-mechanism position, not a measured cost-saving number.
